data.title: Omada Health: Business Is Scaling Very Healthily
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Omada Health (OMDA) was a buy rating because the key data points like membership growth, adoption, and GLP-1 Care Track were all moving in the right direction. My buy call on OMDA remains intact, as the Q3 validated my thesis. Revenue grew 50% y/y to $68 million, membership hit 831,000, and gross margin expanded to 68.2%, bringing OMDA within reach of its long-term margin target. With prescribing now added to the GLP-1 strategy and cross-sell adoption jumping to ~75%, OMDA is scaling really well.

Q3 2025 earnings recap

This is for the benefit of readers that have not caught up with OMDA's latest results. For Q3, revenue came in at $68 million, up 50% y/y, driven by membership increasing to 831,000, up 53% y/y, which marks an acceleration from 47% in Q1. By mix, services revenue grew 50% to $62.9 million, while hardware grew 49% to $5.1 million. For hardware, the growth is basically tied to the growth in new members since each enrollment would trigger a connected device shipment. The way to assess these numbers is to view each device as a setup for long-term recurring service revenue. The margin story was also great. Adj. gross margin expanded ~350 bps y/y to 68.2% (recall that I have talked about margins moving toward 70%; OMDA is essentially there), which drove adj. EBITDA to $2.4 million, marking OMDA's first positive result.

GLP-1 Strategy

Back in September, I saw this GLP-1 strategy as more of a bridge for OMDA to win contracts because the program had strong clinical backing. But now believe the strategic outlook is even better. OMDA is now adding prescribing capabilities, and I think this fundamentally changes things. Previously, OMDA supported members already taking GLP-1s. But with licensed providers now able to prescribe and manage these medications, OMDA has extended its presence in the entire therapy cycle.

This is a meaningful development because the GLP-1 landscape is getting more complex. There are just so many variables to deal with, such as injectables, orals, and even next-generation single, dual, and tri-agonists. And they all come with different price points and clinical profiles. From the typical company point of view, the lack of knowledge (which drug and medium to choose) means the cost exposure is huge, especially if the wrong drugs are used, which results in suboptimal results. With OMDA prescribing capabilities, clients should see direct improvements in ROI from choosing the right medication choice (right drug = better patient results = less recurring needs = lower cost = higher ROI).

The competitive implications are significant. This move shifts OMDA from being a typical wellness solution vendor to one that further entrenches itself into clients' operations. In other words, it tightens retention. Management also noted strong inbound interest from employers wanting these capabilities, which tells me the market wants such a solution that integrates behavioral, clinical, and prescription management (entire lifecycle management, basically).

Multi-Condition Adoption

OMDA's shift toward a multi-condition platform is moving much faster than I expected. In my last update, I pointed out that 31% of customers were using more than one program exiting 2024. We now have real solid proof that this strategy has scaled extremely well. As of Q3, ~75% of new customers year-to-date are adopting more than one program, which is a sharp step up from the 50% rate for 2024 (and also well above the 31% installed-base figure).

This has a direct impact on OMDA's financial model. Firstly, its operating leverage. The idea is simple here. Cross-selling a second or third program costs far less than acquiring a new customer. Also, when 75% of new deals include multiple products, it also means that each sales cycle effectively delivers 2-3x the revenue for similar effort (to acquire a new customer). This can be well seen in the sales and marketing figure, which, as a percentage of revenue, fell meaningfully y/y.

Secondly, this effectively increases OMDA's competitive edge. This is also straightforward. If I were the employer. I naturally would not want to be dealing with a dozen point solutions that each cover different verticals (diabetes, hypertension, etc.) separately. It would make my life a lot simpler to consolidate all these, as I will only have one vendor to deal with, and I can argue for a better price. So, for OMDA, which has a portfolio of offerings, it makes sense that OMDA wins share vs. point solution providers, and the more it wins (new logo + more programs adopted per customer), the lesser the likelihood for clients to churn.

Valuation

The strong Q3 performance was followed by management lifting FY25 revenue by $15 million at the midpoint to $251.5 to $254.5 million, which works out to ~49% y/y growth (similar to Q3's growth). The bigger takeaway here, I think, is that the demand momentum remains solid. For management to guide us when it already has 5 weeks of data (from Q4), it gives confidence that this guide is achievable.

Looking ahead, my previous model assumed revenue growth would decelerate to 30% by FY27, but that now feels too conservative. The exit growth rate for FY25 is close to 50% if we use management's guide, and if we combine the new catalysts like GLP-1 prescribing and faster multi-condition adoption, I think they support a view that growth can land well above 30%. To reflect this, I upgraded my growth assumption by 500 bps each year, 40% in FY26 and 35% in FY27.

Risk

While the outlook is strong, management has noted FY26 as an investment year, which may drag down the pace of margin expansion as OMDA builds out the infrastructure to support GLP-1 prescribing. One additional risk is tariffs, as OMDA's hardware, like connected scales, is largely manufactured in China.

Conclusion

I believe OMDA is entering FY26 with strong momentum. Cross-sell adoption is rising, and the new prescribing capability strengthens the growth outlook. Management's raised FY25 guide also shows that momentum is continuing, not stalling. While FY26 will require investment to support GLP-1 prescribing and could temper near-term margins, I believe the long-term setup is still attractive. I reiterate my buy rating for OMDA.

data.title: Santacruz Silver Mining: The Cigar Butt That Sparked Again (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Since I last covered Santacruz Silver Mining Ltd (OTCPK:SCZMF) with a Hold rating, shares are up 13.25% versus 5.17% for the S&P 500.

I am upgrading my rating to a Buy, but make no mistake—this is a catalyst and momentum driven call, not a vote on quality. Santacruz is preparing for a Nasdaq listing, and with a 1.4x beta to silver over the past three years, I think the stock offers meaningful upside if silver keeps rising.

Silver Running In First Place

Silver has had a strong run recently, with the Comex Silver Active contract closing last week at $57.16. The metal has held above its 50-day moving average, and the uptrend still looks intact. The MACD on the six-month chart recently gave a bullish crossover, while the RSI remains firmly in bullish territory, though it is starting to edge toward overbought levels.

The widely referenced gold/silver ratio shows that silver has caught up to gold, and on a relative basis, has even run ahead recently. Looking at the five year average—which I like to use since it captures the start of the recent precious metals rally in February 2022, after Russia invaded Ukraine—the ratio sits at 82.28 on average compared with 75.44 today. This suggests to me that silver's catch-up move, given its higher beta relative to gold, has largely played out. While I think there could still be some upside for silver, I expect the pace of the rally to be less extreme from here, with gold likely starting to catch up on a relative basis.

Q3 Earnings

Santacruz reported Q3 Earnings on the 27th of November and the results read like a reminder that mining always throws up unexpected complications. The headline story this quarter is obviously the lingering impact of the flooding event at the Bolivar asset back in May—a development that knocked the wind out of what had been the company's highest grade silver source. Silver production at Bolivar fell 57% quarter-over-quarter, and 73% year-over-year. The flooding meant that Bolivar could not access its high-grade Pomabamba and Nané zones, which normally drive the mine's standout silver grades. Management says that rehabilitation and dewatering have been progressing steadily at Bolivar, and that these higher-grade areas are expected to be accessible by February 2026 again—and that full recovery is anticipated by the fourth quarter of next year. If you just look at production tables, the hit looks dramatic—especially if you consider that production for Bolivar is shown on a 100% basis, while Santacruz owns just 45% of the asset—something that is easy to miss when just glancing over the production tables. When you step back and think about it some more, Q3 ends up saying more about how the rest of the portfolio matured and how much more resilient Santacruz has become operationally and financially.

Lets start with the fundamentals. The company reported almost $80 million in revenue, basically flat year-over-year despite selling meaningfully fewer silver ounces. That disconnect is almost entirely a function of the silver price, which averaged over $40 in the quarter versus under $30 this time last year. That price tailwind cushioned the blow from Bolivar, and without it, the quarter would have looked a lot softer.

What made this quarter respectable is everything outside of Bolivar. Caballo Blanco posted year-over-year gains in silver production and benefited from improved recoveries thanks to a deliberate change in how ore is blended through the Don Diego Mill. San Lucas—often overlooked because it is a feed-sourcing business rather than a traditional mine—once again proved its value, in my view. It kept mills running at high utilisation and essentially acted as a shock absorber for the group while the Bolivar asset stumbled. And over in Mexico, Zimapan continues to show the benefit of last year's capex. The results aren't spectacular, but steady, with modest gains in zinc and copper and more consistent throughput.

On the costs side...AISC did rise sharply, but was largely due to factors outside management's control. AISC climbed to $35.62 per ounce, a big step up from $22.95 per ounce in Q2 or $27.40 per ounce in Q3'24. The main culprit was overwhelmingly Bolivar. With high-grade zones underwater and out of reach, the mine was forced into lower-quality material while simultaneously absorbing the direct cost of a major dewatering campaign (for more colour... Bolivar's own AISC blew out to a staggering $50.89 per ounce). The strengthening Bolivian currency also didn't help either. Ironically, an improving macro backdrop in Bolivia raises Santacruz's USD costs.

Despite all of this, I think the more important development this quarter was around the balance sheet. On the 5th of September, Santacruz made the final payment to Glencore and officially closed the book on the $80 million Bolivian acquisition liability. They did it early, for a negotiated sum of $40 million in cash, wiping out a major overhang that has shadowed Santacruz for years. With that now gone, I think the company is in a much cleaner financial position heading into 2026.

So to summaries my views on Q3, don't subscribe to the "production down, costs up" narrative while missing the fact that Santacruz absorbed a meaningful operational setback at its most important mine but still managed to report higher profit, stronger margins, and a significantly de-risked balance sheet. And once Bolivar's high-grade zones come back online—likely early next year—Santacruz regains the grade profile that made it compelling in the first place. Layer a firm silver price on top of that, and I think things are looking up for Santacruz heading into 2026.

Risks

In my opinion, the main risk with Santacruz remains limited visibility around future production. On paper, several core assets have only a few years of mine life left, and production at these assets have been trending lower in recent years. Yes, narrow-vein mining has a long history of extending mine lives beyond what the reserve tables suggest—but extensions are never guaranteed, and investors are effectively being asked to trust future drilling and development rather than hard reserves. Add to than Santacruz's uneven filing history in recent years, including late reports, and it is clear that Santacruz is not a polished by-the-book operator just yet. None of this negates the fact that Santacruz is a high beta play on the silver price, but reinforces why I think it remains a higher-risk, lower-visibility story that can reward risk-seeking investors—and just as easily punish complacency if you don't risk-manage your trade properly.

Valuation

For those familiar with my work, you know I usually rely on the Blended Forward EV/EBITDA ratios from the Bloomberg Terminal to best gauge what I am paying for a miner's operations relative to the earnings they are forecast to generate.

Santacruz, however, is a bit off the radar, so the data simply isn't available there. Therefore, I am leaning heavily on Seeking Alpha's Quant ratings and some third-party research from Atrium to get a sense of valuation and relative value versus peers.

Santacruz has an A- Seeking Alpha Quant valuation rating. The FWD EV/EBITDA ratio of 5.1x is 43% below the sector median. However, I think investors should still account for the fact that Santacruz has a portfolio of low-lived, low-visibility assets, and for that reason it should be trading at a bit of a discount... but maybe not at such a big discount to where it is currently trading at.

Atrium Research has a price target of C$3.10 (29% above the current share price) which is based on an expected 2025 OCF of $96.7 million and an 8.5x EV/CF multiple.

The Technical Take

On the technicals, the setup looks really solid. After pulling back from recent all-time highs, Santacruz shares found support at a key confluence—the 100-day moving average and the 50% Fibonacci retracement level. The MACD indicator is flashing a bullish signal, while the RSI is also firmly in bullish territory. I also like that volume recently saw a big tick up again.

Takeaway

I am upgrading Santacruz Silver to a Buy, and concur with Atrium Research's C$3.10 price target, which implies roughly 29% upside from current levels. This rating reflects the potential for a momentum-driven trade, supported by the Nasdaq listing preparation, operational catalysts, and the stock's 1.4x beta to silver over the past three years. I am certainly not calling Santacruz high quality—it remains short-lived with limited production visibility—but if silver continues to rally and important operational and corporate catalysts play out, I think the stock could deliver meaningful upside for investors. Because this is very much a catalyst and momentum based play, the trade should be approached with appropriate risk management. Any break below the 100-day moving average and the 50% Fibonacci retracement level would be enough reason for me to close out my position.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 2026 Perspectives: Discipline In An Age Of Disruption
data.description:
By Kamal Bhatia, CFA, Chief Executive Officer

Executive letter

As we enter 2026, let’s reflect on the journey we’ve navigated together over the past year—marked by both challenges and opportunities in a rapidly evolving landscape. The resilience of the global economy gives us plenty to be optimistic about as we move forward.

At Principal Asset Management, our dedication to collaboration remains at the forefront of our mission. We understand that the current market environment demands a proactive and nuanced investment strategy. With 360-degree expertise across equities, fixed income, asset allocation, real estate, and private markets, we are prepared to help you seize opportunities as they arise and manage risks effectively.

In the face of ongoing economic shifts, having trusted partners to help distinguish between signals and noise is critical. Our teams continuously analyze market trends and leverage local insights, enabling us to tailor investment strategies that align with your evolving needs. Together, we aim to identify high-potential asset classes, sectors, and ultimately securities while navigating volatility and uncertainty.

As we move ahead into the new year, integrating innovative solutions into our investment strategies will be crucial. We are committed to delivering forward-looking insights and diversified portfolios that are designed to maximize your returns, regardless of market conditions.

Thank you for your ongoing trust in Principal Asset Management. We are excited about the prospects 2026 holds and remain committed to partnering with you to achieve your investment goals.

By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist

Macro - The year of the paradox

2025 will be remembered as a year when policy upheaval tested the resilience of the global economy in ways few anticipated. Trade tensions deepened, inflationary pressures lingered, and geopolitical fractures widened. Yet, against this backdrop of uncertainty, growth endured—powered by strong balance sheets, agile policy responses, and the accelerating march of artificial intelligence. These forces not only defined 2025, but they will also shape the contours of 2026.

Growth endures despite global disruption

The United States sits at the epicenter of both global growth hopes and concerns. The recent government shutdown has clouded near-term visibility, but the economy’s underlying architecture remains intact. Corporate profit margins have held firm, enabling businesses to pivot through disruption, while household wealth gains have secured household balance sheets and sustained consumer spending.

Beneath these familiar pillars lies a new engine: AI infrastructure investment. In the first half of 2025, AI-related capital expenditure accounted for nearly half of GDP growth—a staggering figure that underscores the scale of this structural shift. With technology firms showing no appetite to rein in spending, AI will remain a dominant force in the year ahead—although mounting concerns over a potential bubble may limit its ability to propel equity markets and household wealth.

Fiscal policy will add a layer of complexity in 2026. The “One Big Beautiful Bill” promises a short-lived but potent stimulus: tax refunds for households and retroactive incentives for corporates that could temporarily lower effective tax rates and unlock free cash flow. While the direct impact may fade quickly, the investment impulse it triggers could reverberate well beyond the initial fiscal lift. This combination of fiscal support and structural investment creates a powerful backdrop for growth, even as cyclical uncertainties persist.

Fault lines in a late-cycle economy

Yet, the U.S. economy is not without fault lines. Its K-shaped nature—where affluent households drive consumption while lower-income cohorts struggle—poses a structural challenge even if the aggregate impact remains muted. As the mid-term election year approaches, the Trump administration is paying closer attention to these lower-income segments, seeking to ease affordability pressures through potential cash transfers and more lenient tariff policies in select sectors. While fiscal constraints cast doubt on full implementation, the government’s attention to these strained pockets of the economy is likely to endure.

Labor market dynamics will also warrant close attention: hiring has slowed, but layoffs remain contained, and unemployment is near historic lows. Labor demand is slowing, but shifting labor supply dynamics, such as tighter immigration policies, also appear to be in play. Put differently, reduced hiring signals a cooling economy, but not one on the brink of a labor market unravelling. AI adoption could also reshape employment over time, but in the near term, firms view it as a growth catalyst rather than a threat. The longer-term implications for labor displacement and wage dynamics, however, remain an open question.

For the Federal Reserve, the interplay of AI-driven investment, fiscal stimulus, and labor supply dynamics points to only a modest easing cycle. Furthermore, inflation remains above target, and the full impact of tariffs on price pressures is still unfolding – warranting a cautious approach. The Fed’s key challenge in 2026 will be to calibrate policy in an environment where structural forces, rather than cyclical ones, increasingly drive growth and inflation dynamics.

Nuanced, but stabilizing global outlook

Globally, the picture is equally nuanced. Trade tensions will persist, shaping alliances and supply chains. China has defied fears of an export recession, with supply chains increasingly diversifying away from the U.S. and towards intra-Asian linkages. It has also stepped up efforts to dominate the next wave of technological innovation, positioning the economy as a key beneficiary of the AI narrative. This willingness to lean even deeper into its external strengths and tech innovation will be important as domestic demand continues to falter amid measured policy stimulus attempts.

China’s determination to preserve its current account surplus while reducing reliance on the U.S. poses a challenge for Europe. Heightened competition for export markets threatens Europe’s manufacturing sector and could introduce mild deflationary pressures. However, easing financial conditions and Germany’s long-awaited departure from fiscal austerity should support economic activity. Europe may not emerge as a standout performer in 2026; still, its solid macro backdrop and relatively low tech exposure—amid concerns of an AI-driven bubble— are likely to appeal to many investors.

In sum, the macro outlook is constructive, but late-cycle dynamics are unmistakable. AI has been the dominant driver of recent market gains, yet questions around valuations, productivity payoffs, and energy constraints will fuel volatility. Rising debt-funded investment and increasingly circular deal-making add to the sense of fragility, even as innovation continues to inspire. The narrative of "AI as the productivity gamechanger" is compelling, but it is also vulnerable to shifts in sentiment, particularly as investors increasingly demand stronger returns on investment.

2026 will be a year of paradox: lifted by innovation and macro resilience, markets may brim with optimism even as questions about the durability of tech-driven gains cast a subtle shadow. If macro foundations hold, markets can continue to climb the wall of worry. Yet, in an otherwise benign global backdrop, technology—particularly AI—stands as both the greatest opportunity and the greatest source of uncertainty. Investors must stay positioned for transformative growth while mitigating concentration risks amid rising skepticism. The challenge will be to balance conviction with caution, embracing innovation without overlooking the late-cycle warning signals.

By George Maris, CFA, Chief Investment Officer, Global Equities

Equities - Equities in transition

Heading into 2026, the equity markets are navigating a complex landscape characterized by strong performance, significant liquidity, and the transformative impact of artificial intelligence (AI). Yet, the forces shaping returns, from liquidity and momentum toward fundamentals, earnings visibility, and policy normalization, are shifting. Staying disciplined after 2025’s market rally, while remaining aware of these dynamics, is important for positioning for the year ahead.

A key driver of this bullish sentiment is enthusiasm surrounding AI technologies. Firms producing and underpinning AI software, along with companies leveraging AI in their operations, saw their valuations soar, at times without clear indications of how these investments will translate into future profitability. This exuberance, while understandable given the incredible benefits of this innovation, raises concerns about potential overvaluation as the market may exhibit an overly optimistic bias.

In 2026, the AI narrative is likely to evolve from story to execution. Investors will need to differentiate between firms realizing measurable productivity gains and those still chasing an ethereal potential.

Investment discipline and portfolio construction

In this evolving environment of lofty, liquidity-driven AI-related valuations, managers must adopt a disciplined approach that emphasizes fundamentals over story-driven trends. This includes conducting thorough underwriting to ensure investment cases are sound and asking hard questions about valuations predicated on future earnings potential. A scalpel approach—allocating with precision to companies showing cash flow-derived value—is as essential now as was the case for decades.

Concerns over market volatility

Market volatility poses a significant challenge for investors. Recent dynamics led to shorter, more violent periods of volatility, driven by market structure changes such as the dramatic rise of retail trading, options activity, and exchange-traded funds (ETFs). These elements contribute to increased market noise-inducing volatility, making it essential for investors to remain vigilant and adaptable.

As always, the current market environment presents both opportunities and risks. While abundant liquidity helps drive prices higher, we regularly witness how rapid shifts in sentiment lead to violent price movements. Investors need to be prepared for corrections while staying focused on long-term fundamentals. This requires balancing risk management with fundamentally oriented conviction: staying positioned for potential corrections while remaining ready to seize opportunities emerging from volatility.

Global perspectives

Japan and the UK currently present opposing trajectories. Japan continues to benefit from structural reforms and improved sovereign and corporate governance, while the UK struggles with stagnation and policy inconsistency. Yet both markets share a common feature: large exporter bases whose relative valuations (Japan’s far exceeding that in the UK) diverge more than fundamentals suggest. Active selection remains key.

Emerging markets, particularly in Asia, are also intriguing. While specific markets such as China, South Korea, Brazil and Poland have outperformed and remain attractive, consideration is required to navigate the complexities of each region's economic, political, and corporate landscape. The rise of technology and digital transformation in these markets presents both opportunities for growth and risks associated with geopolitical tensions. 2026 will test investor appetite amid shifting fiscal policies in major economies and evolving geopolitics.

Sector insights go far beyond AI

Looking across sectors, a few themes are particularly worth highlighting:

  • Financials, particularly banks, continue to benefit from higher-for-longer rate dynamics, especially in the U.S.
  • Healthcare, especially biotechnology, lagged over the prior year but now offers attractive opportunities, especially as merger and acquisition activity escalates.
  • Consumer sectors are worth watching. While tariffs present a challenging dynamic, as tariff impacts ease, the recently enacted U.S. fiscal package should be a shot in the arm for consumer and corporate spending.
  • The materials and energy spaces are also alluring, led by copper, which looks attractive from both a secular and a cyclical perspective, as industry continues to consolidate.

Across sectors, an opportunity lies in companies translating policy tailwinds and AI-enabled efficiency into sustained earnings.

The path ahead points to the importance of a disciplined approach

As investors enter 2026, the challenge will be maintaining discipline in a story-driven and volatile environment. Equity markets are evolving, driven by enthusiasm and liquidity, and while the coming year offers opportunity, there is also the risk of overvaluation and resultant volatility.

Success in 2026 depends on emphasizing forward free cash generation. The warranted excitement around AI will likely turn to a focus of its impact on productivity, profits, and growth. Investors who stay selective, balancing discipline with agility, are well-positioned to navigate uncertainty and capture the opportunities ahead.

By Michael Goosay, Chief Investment Officer, Global Fixed Income

Fixed income - Adaptability is paramount

Reflecting on 2025, investors have encountered uncertainty fueled by tariffs, geopolitical conflicts in Europe and the Middle East, and the implications of the One Big Beautiful Bill. Yet, amidst these challenges, the U.S. economy displayed surprising resilience, supported by strong consumer spending and corporate profits, which ultimately laid the groundwork for positive market performance.

Looking ahead to 2026, the fixed income landscape presents a cautiously constructive backdrop. While inflation is expected to remain above the Fed’s 2% target for the foreseeable future, the central bank is likely to gradually adjust interest rates downward toward a neutral level, projected to be around 3%. Although achieving this target is a priority, the Fed will likely tolerate some inflation beyond this level without compromising price stability. Against this backdrop, the opportunity for attractive income and total return remains, especially for active investors prepared to navigate sector dispersion and policy nuance.

Macro dynamics supporting the outlook

The trajectory of the Fed's policy will be crucial in defining the fixed income landscape in 2026. We expect the Fed to begin gradually lowering rates by mid-year as it seeks to balance persistent inflationary pressures with a cooling—but not collapsing—labor market. This gradual recalibration is essential for fostering sustained economic growth, even in the face of sticky but steady inflationary pressures. Market expectations already reflect a more accommodative approach to policy changes sooner than previously anticipated, as the Fed effectively balances its dual mandates of stable prices and full employment.

Yield curve and duration positioning

As the Fed moves to lower interest rates further, there is potential for a steeper curve, which is consistent with prior easing cycles. Such environments typically lead to appreciation across the yield curve, offering investors opportunities to capture gains alongside income generation.

Fixed income yield levels

Yield to worst, last ten years

We remain moderately long in duration, emphasizing flexibility to respond to curve shifts and evolving inflation expectations. Effectively managing exposure across both ends of the curve will be key to optimizing returns in this interest rate environment.

Sector views: Staying opportunistic and selective

High yield

High yield remains particularly attractive due to its limited potential for spread widening or further tightening. Despite economic uncertainty, the credit quality has improved markedly, with a reduction in the number of lower-rated issuers, which has helped tighten spreads. In fact, the percentage of CCC-rated securities fell to just 10% of the index, which justifies spread levels, and positions high yield as a compelling carry trade in a low-interest-rate context.

New issuance was heavy in 2025, and we expect that to continue. The market has easily absorbed this paper, but eventually the risk of supply exceeding demand will grow. So, while still overweight, we are biased toward a neutral position.

Investment grade

Investment-grade credit continues to be viewed favorably, supported by strong technicals, solid fundamentals, and a favorable macro environment. However, tighter spreads present a more complicated landscape. Although demand for corporate debt and high-quality paper will likely persist, new supply may eventually outpace demand as firms take advantage of the Fed’s easing cycle to step up issuance. Moreover, consumer-linked credits may face challenges should the labor market weaken. Overall, IG credit remains resilient, allowing active managers to leverage bottom-up credit selection and sector differentiation effectively. Nevertheless, potential upticks in supply may weigh on the sector.

Emerging markets

Emerging markets present another area of interest, characterized by unique regional stories, opportunities and risks. With a positive outlook for certain segments driven by declining global rates, emerging market debt appears promising. But it is crucial for investors to focus on regional strengths and sector-specific dynamics rather than adopting a blanket bullish approach. As an active manager, we seek to take advantage of individual markets, both dollar-denominated and local currency. Looking ahead, there are opportunities in the sector for an agile active manager, and we remain overweight.

Municipal bonds

Municipal bonds have underperformed other fixed income sectors through most of 2025, resulting in a very attractive risk/return profile, particularly on a tax-adjusted basis. Heading into next year, we’re in an environment where many coastal states, and parts of the Midwest are raising tax rates, and that's likely to continue. That, in turn, should feed demand and drive attractive returns in the municipal bond space. Within Munis, we tend to avoid general obligation assets and focus more on revenue bonds and infrastructure projects where there are opportunities to extract value from an alpha perspective, while reducing risk.

A favorable outlook, but the ability to adapt is key

The fixed income market is characterized by a delicate interplay of factors that require careful navigation. Investors should remain vigilant regarding inflation trends and the Federal Reserve's policy adjustments, as these will significantly influence the yield curve and sector performance. Yet this environment bodes well for fixed income investments. Investors can benefit from more favorable income rates compared to the ultra-low levels of a few years ago. This holds the potential for reasonable total returns in 2026.

By Todd Jablonski, CFA, Chief Investment Officer, Multi-asset & Quant Strategies

Multi-asset - The year of tactical investments

Is the specter of a rewarding 2026 with plenty of successful investment opportunities too good to be true? Interestingly, many seem to think so. Investor sentiment is on the wane amid concerns over sluggish U.S. growth, persistent ~3% inflation, cooling labor markets, and renewed geopolitical and trade tensions. Yet, there remain a large number of reasons to be optimistic about the year ahead. The U.S. economy is showing resilience in key areas, notably in corporate earnings growth and in the higher-end consumer economy. Taken in total, the risk-on vs. risk-off decision for asset allocation is opaque entering 2026. Our research guides us to “cautiously risk on” in 2026, where we expect tactical asset allocation decisions to be critical to investment success.

Valuation pressure meetings sentiment caution

For the investors on “team risk off,” the concern seems to come from all-time low risk premia. Markets have been on a roll the last several years, starting with 2023’s +26.3% total return from the S&P 500, followed by 2024’s +25.0% total return, and 2025’s +17.5% total return through October. In short, U.S. stocks are up by more than +85% in less than three years, and investors are wary the good times can continue.

Stocks are near universally “expensive” all over the globe despite great growth in the “E” denominator in the P/E fraction. Outside of a few pockets in Latin America, Southeast Asia, and U.S. REITs, value is extraordinarily difficult to find. To boot, a weakening dollar and abrupt policy shifts also seem to have eroded foreign investors’ confidence in U.S. assets. Developed ex-U.S. markets posted a +26.6% net total return in 2025 through October, while gold, silver, and cryptocurrencies (dollar alternatives) have reached new highs.

Meanwhile, in the bond market, credit spreads are historically tight (read: expensive) despite a spate of well-publicized bankruptcies in 2025 and murmurings of waning credit quality. We view the tight risk premia as adding to market fragility, as any combination of slowing consumer spending, weakening consumer credit, AI profitability delays, or rising tax rates in developed countries could frustrate risk assets.

Reasons for optimism in 2026

Despite a lengthy list of potential threats to the outlook, we enter 2026 with optimism and an overweight to equities, credit, and other anti-fragile assets. “Team risk on” can also point to compelling macro forces on its roster. One, momentum is a powerful investment force, and it’s pointed in the right direction. Two, corporate fundamentals were great in 2025, and they appear poised to accelerate. 2026 consensus expectations for global stocks are for +6% sales growth (up from +5% in 2025) and +13% earnings growth (up from +11% in 2025), fueled by sharply positive earnings revisions the past twelve months. Three, many investors seem too eager for too many Fed cuts, failing to appreciate the positive impacts of easing financial conditions that arise from even modest easing. We expect a modest Fed easing cycle in 2026, and the “Fed put” is there for investors should a negative shock damage the already-mixed economic outlook.

U.S. primary balance and Fed funds rate

U.S. primary balance % of GDP and Fed funds rate, recession are shaded, March 1980–present

Finally, and perhaps most importantly, fiscal and monetary policy in the U.S. is in a unique, supportive position for investors. The United States federal government today is spending massively on economic growth, a stimulative fiscal approach typically reserved for recession escape. Simultaneously, the Federal Reserve is in the midst of an easing cycle, with a dominant eye on the full employment side of the ledger, a stimulative monetary approach typically reserved for recession escape. Thus, both U.S. fiscal policy and monetary policy are in expansive mode…and we are not in a recession! The rare combination of expansive policies absent a recession is an environment where stocks, credit, and other risk assets have historically performed well.

A year for active positioning

We also expect 2026 to be a critical year for asset allocation risk and asset allocation reward. When macro forces loom large, narrow idiosyncratic issues take a back seat to investment agility and dynamism. Buying opportunities are likely to arise as investors overreact to high frequency data, guess at the size and speed of Fed cuts, and project when the AI capex theme will shift from “expense” to “corporate profit center.” We encourage increased liquidity and preparedness for the year ahead.

By Todd Everett, Global Head of Private Markets

Private markets - Conditions align for real assets and private credit

In 2025, private markets responded with resilience to geopolitical and macroeconomic shocks. While sentiment wavered at times, momentum recovered quickly, reinforcing long-term confidence in the asset class. With the initial wave of shifts in U.S. governmental policies, trade negotiations, and impact on economic growth now more transparent, there are several factors emerging that are likely to shape private market performance in 2026.

Overall, the outlook is constructive for investment across the asset class and risk spectrum, but there are many considerations (both positive and cautionary) that investors should keep in mind.

Cost of leverage declines

Leverage, a critical driver of private market investment, is becoming less expensive. The long end of the U.S. Treasury curve may remain range bound, providing a milder real estate valuation recovery than after previous periods of material devaluation. However, commercial real estate appreciation has generally redeveloped and improving core and core plus returns are expected. In private credit, accommodative Fed actions may reduce the pressure building in below-investment-grade sectors, particularly around the allowance of payment-in-kind (PIK) provisions and rising defaults. For private equity, easing capital costs may unlock liquidity and support exit activity in 2026.

A return to real estate appreciation

The end of the Fed rate hiking cycle coupled with stable debt markets should continue to support capital flows into the real estate sector. In addition, slowing real estate construction deliveries and strengthening space absorption should lead accelerate landlord pricing power across most U.S. property types. While the U.S. leads in sector recovery, Europe also shows promise, supported by targeted fiscal stimulus. Data centers and residential assets remain standout global segments, driven by structural demand trends.

An AI-driven infrastructure acceleration

Infrastructure fundamentals remain robust, with digital and energy sectors benefitting from long-term structural demand. The AI and cloud computing boom is projected to more than double U.S. data center electricity usage by 2030, straining grids and spurring investment across generation, transmission, and storage.

Additionally, shifts in U.S. policy—particularly the transition from the Inflation Reduction Act to the OBBB Act—may compress the clean energy tax credit window, resulting in a spike in activity through 2026 and then a drop-off or slower development post-2027.

Shifting control of the private credit market

Private lenders continue to take market share from traditional banks, particularly in the direct lending and real estate debt sectors. Regulatory shifts and less stringent oversight may also support an uptick in M&A activity, expanding the investable opportunity set. Notably, intense competition in the upper middle market is compressing spreads and loosening terms. By contrast, the lower middle market offers more attractive structures, higher yields, and favorable covenants, especially for first-lien senior secured exposure.

Assessing ABF investment

Asset-backed finance (ABF) is evolving rapidly, with increasingly complex risk transfers targeting wealth and institutional channels. While the relative investment spreads, credit ratings, and short-durational profile of ABF are appealing, it’s essential to understand the underlying performance drivers, modeling of stressed environments, and loss potential in these complex structured investments. Hard asset-backed ABF transactions may offer appealing risk-adjusted opportunities, provided underwriting discipline is maintained.

A constructive environment for allocation

The global alternative investment universe is expected to expand from $21.6 trillion in 2024 to$34.2 trillion by 2030, according to PwC. That’s nearly an 8% compound annual growth rate. Growth does not occur at this level without the belief that the sector will deliver anticipated portfolio benefits including premium risk-adjusted returns, limited volatility, and a low correlation with public stocks and bonds.

With that in mind, private markets are well positioned heading into 2026. Structural tailwinds from AI, infrastructure demand, and capital migration from public to private markets continue to support fundamentals. While macro and geopolitical uncertainty persist, private assets backed by hard collateral, prudent underwriting, and favorable structural features offer resilience and potential upside. Across real assets and private credit, we see opportunities across the risk spectrum for long-term investors seeking diversification, income, and stability.

Private Market Sector Snapshot

Why now Risks to monitor

Private Real Estate

(Equity & Debt)
  • Valuations have corrected ~20%; transaction volumes are rebounding.
  • Supply/demand balance improving in most sectors (ex-office).
  • Opportunities exist to acquire assets below replacement cost.
  • Real estate debt offers competitive total return versus core equity.
  • Recovery is likely to be uneven; returns may not match prior cycles.
  • Cap rate spreads remain modest in favored sectors.
  • Slower labor markets may pressure demand in weaker geographies.

Private Infrastructure (Equity & Debt)
  • Strong demand drivers across digital, energy, and transport sectors.
  • Historically low correlation with public markets enhances diversification.
  • Government funding gaps create tailwinds for private capital.
  • Clean energy development may slow post-2026 as policy incentives shift.
  • Project timelines may be pulled forward, potentially creating a development lull after 2027.

Private High Yield Credit / Direct Lending
  • Public market spreads have compressed, enhancing the relative value of direct lending.
  • Corporate fundamentals remain strong, defaults below long-term averages.
  • Lower middle market continues to offer structural advantages.
  • PIK issuance remains elevated (~7.2% of index).
  • Manager dispersion is widening; underwriting discipline is key.

Private Investment-Grade Debt
  • Issuance volumes remain strong; investor protections are holding.
  • Yield premiums over comparable public IG bonds remain attractive.
  • Institutional mandates can now build global, diversified exposures.
  • Sector and regional diversification remain critical amid macro uncertainty.

Fixed income yield chart index descriptions:

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index represents securities that are SEC-registered, taxable, and dollar-denominated. The index covers the U.S. investment-grade fixed rate bond market, with index components for government and corporate securities, mortgage pass-through securities, and asset-backed securities. These major sectors are subdivided into more specific indices that are calculated and reported on a regular basis.

The Bloomberg CMBS ERISA-Eligible Index is the ERISA-eligible component of the Bloomberg CMBS Index. This index, which includes investment-grade securities that are ERISA eligible under the underwriter’s exemption, is the only CMBS sector that is included in the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index.

The Bloomberg Asset-Backed Securities (ABS) Index has three subsectors: Credit and charge cards, Autos, Utility. The index includes pass-through, bullet, and controlled amortization structures. The ABS Index includes only the senior class of each ABS issue and the ERISA-eligible B and C tranche. The Manufactured Housing sector was removed as of January 1, 2008, and the Home Equity Loan sector was removed as of October 1, 2009.

The S&P/LSTA (Loan Syndications and Trading Association) Leveraged Loan 100 Index is designed to measure the performance of the U.S. leveraged loan market based upon market weightings, spreads, and interest payments. The index is composed of loans bought by institutional investors that have partnered with S&P Global Market Intelligence’s Leveraged Commentary & Data (LCD). Index constituents are market-value weighted, subject to a single loan facility weight cap of 2%.

Bloomberg U.S. Corp HY 2% Issuer Capped is the 2% Issuer Cap component of the U.S. Corporate High Yield index. The Bloomberg U.S. High Yield Index covers the universe of fixed rate, non-investment-grade debt. Eurobonds and debt issues from countries designated as emerging markets (sovereign rating of Baa1/BBB+/BBB+ and below using the middle of Moody’s, S&P, and Fitch) are excluded, but Canadian and global bonds (SEC registered) of issuers in non-EMG countries are included. Original issue zeroes, step-up coupon structures, 144-As and pay-in-kind bonds (PIKs, as of October 1, 2009) are also included.

The Bloomberg U.S. Credit Index is the U.S. Credit component of the U.S. Government/Credit index and includes publicly issued U.S. corporate and foreign debentures and secured notes that meet specified maturity, liquidity, and quality requirements.

The Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Index is public obligations of the U.S. Treasury with a remaining maturity of one year or more.

Bloomberg U.S. MBS Index covers the mortgage-backed pass-through securities of Ginnie Mae (GNMA), Fannie Mae (FNMA), and Freddie Mac (FHLMC). The MBS Index is formed by grouping the universe of over 600,000 individual fixed rate MBS pools into approximately 3,500 generic aggregates.

The Bloomberg U.S. Municipal Bond Index is a rules-based, market-value-weighted index engineered for the long-term tax-exempt bond market.

The J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Diversified Index (EMBIGD) tracks liquid, US Dollar emerging market fixed and floating-rate debt instruments issued by sovereign and quasi-sovereign entities. The index was launched in July 1999 with daily historical index levels dating back to December 1993. Historical to-maturity and to-worst statistics are available from December 1997 and December 2001, respectively.

data.title: ETY: Suitable For Investors Seeking Consistent Income
data.description:
Overview

With the growing popularity of option ETFs, investors may forget that these sorts of funds have been around for a long time. For instance, Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Diversified Equity Income Fund (ETY) has an inception dating back to 2006, which means we have nearly two decades worth of operating history to reference. The fund has a total expense ratio of 1.06%, and when excluding operating fees, the actual management fee sits at 0.99%. When I previously covered ETY, I issued a buy rating due to the attractive valuation at the time. However, I wanted to revisit the fund to discuss its performance and why it may be a great long-term option for income investors.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that ETY's share price has increased by about 2.9%. The fund has demonstrated its ability to partially participate in the positive momentum of the market. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 11.3% over the same time frame. ETY now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 7.6%, with payouts being issued on a monthly basis. This makes ETY a great option for investors seeking a reliable source of income generation from a diverse pool of equities.

Additionally, the fund has a long history of consistent dividend payouts. Therefore, ETY is a great option for investors that want to utilize a simple buy-and-hold approach while maintaining exposure to some of the highest-quality businesses in the world. However, there are some notable peers that may outperform ETY over the long term. I will cover those funds and briefly discuss the differences in those peers. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that ETY implements to generate its income and earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, ETY has total net assets of $2.5B that are spread across 53 different holdings. Instead of trying to mimic a specific index, ETY chooses to take a more focused approach to its holdings by primarily including dividend-paying stocks. However, the fund's primary objective is to generate current income from its holdings. The fund is almost entirely focused on U.S. equities, but there is a very small exposure to Europe-based securities.

  • North America: 98.72%
  • Europe: 1.28%

In order to achieve its goal of income generation, ETY writes options against the S&P 500 Index to help amplify the income levels received. This allows the fund to capture option premiums, which are then used to fuel the distributions that are paid out to shareholders. The primary drawback to the inclusion of options is that the growth potential of the fund is ultimately capped at whatever the selected strike price is.

When looking at the breakdown of the portfolio, ETY is most heavily allocated to the information technology sector at 36.44% of assets. This is followed by exposure to communication services and financial investments, which account for 14.54% and 13.19% of assets, respectively. Since the top two sectors have a weight of over 50% of the fund's total assets, investors can expect that the daily price movement is impacted by information technology and communication services.

When looking at the holdings of ETY below, we can see ETY provides exposure to some of the highest quality businesses in the world. For instance, Nvidia (NVDA) is the largest holding at 9.27%. This is followed by exposure to many other notable holdings such as Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META), and Broadcom (AVGO), just to name a few. According to the options breakdown, ETY is currently writing options against 48% of the portfolio value, with an average of fourteen days until expiration.

Lastly, ETY is structured as a closed-end fund, so the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, ETY currently trades at a small discount to NAV of 0.89%. For reference, ETY has traded at an average discount to NAV of 0.53% over the last five-year period. Therefore, the larger discount to NAV pricing can be an indication that it's still an attractive time to accumulate shares.

Performance Insights and Risk

Despite the fund providing exposure to some of the highest-quality growth companies in the world, I believe that investors should limit their expectations around the fund's ability to provide attractive price growth. The inclusion of options means that investors are essentially trading future growth potential for income generation now.

To demonstrate this weakness, we can see that ETY's share price has increased by a little more than 37% over the last decade. When including all distributions paid to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 233%. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions.

While these returns are solid, there are some comparable funds that also provide an attractive value proposition for long-term income investors. While these peer funds may have different underlying strategies, holdings, and sector breakdowns, the underlying premise of generating income from equities remains similar. I believe that two of the most compelling peers are the following:

  • BlackRock Enhanced Large Cap Core Fund (CII): ~6.5% dividend yield.
  • Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Opportunities Fund (ETV): ~8.3% dividend yield.

As we can see below, ETY has the largest assets under management at $2.5B despite it having the shortest operating history of the peer group. Conversely, CII has the longest operating history, with an inception dating back to 2004, with assets under management of $1B. Additionally, CII has the lowest expense ratio of the peer group. CII also implements an option writing strategy against its equities, but the smaller payout means that the fund is paying out less of its earnings, which can lead to better NAV growth over time.

ETV is a fund that also operates under the Eaton Vance umbrella, with the primary difference being the fact that ETV writes options against its holdings rather than an index. Besides this one difference, the underlying holdings and breakdown by sector look very similar. In terms of performance, the difference in option writing means that ETV can provide a high yield since the option premiums may be larger. However, this approach of writing options on its underlying holdings means that the growth potential is more limited than ETY's approach.

When measuring the performance of this peer group, we can see that CII has the highest total return of 266.5% over the last ten-year period. ETY's total return follows behind at 223%. Even from a price perspective, CII has the highest growth at 76.5%, which demonstrates its ability to participate in a greater level of capital appreciation over time. This could be attributed to the possibility that CII implements an OTM (out-of-the-money) option strategy against its holdings, which allows for a greater level of capital appreciation to be captured.

Despite CII's outperformance, we could argue that ETY is still a solid choice for investors seeking a higher dividend yield. Some investors may be okay with the potential for a lower total return in exchange for a high level of distributions received. This is most relevant for retired investors that may depend on the income generation within their portfolio as a supplemental source of income to cover lifestyle expenses.

Dividends

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0992 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.6%. When looking back at the dividend payout history below, we can see that ETY has a history of keeping payouts within a consistent range over a long period of time. Since the fund typically fuels distributions through the combination of net investment income, net realized gains, and the option premiums collected, there are no concerns about the overall sustainability of the distributions going forward.

The only downside is that the fund doesn't have the best track record at providing an attractive rate of dividend growth over time. The payouts have mostly remained flat, which means that investors will need to create their own dividend growth. This can be achieved by reinvesting portions of the distributions received or adding new capital on a consistent basis.

However, one of the drawbacks is that the distributions may not be the most tax efficient. For instance, the latest Section 19(a) notice indicates that 40.9% of the distributions paid on a YTD basis have been fueled by net realized short-term gains, which are classified as ordinary dividends and have the highest tax burden for investors. I suspect that this is attributed to the fund selling off gains from the fast-growing technology companies it holds.

So based on your specific tax bracket, desired income level, and the amount of capital invested, a position in ETY may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account. On a more positive note, the remaining bulk of the distributions paid on a YTD basis were made up of net realized long-term gains. These distributions are typically more tax efficient since they are classified as qualified dividends.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on ETY due to the attractive valuation and potential for consistent income generation. The fund's performance has remained attractive over the last decade, and ETY has demonstrated its ability to participate in the growth of the market, despite the included option writing strategy. ETY provides instant exposure to some of the highest quality companies in the world. Although ETY underperforms CII, the higher dividend yield still makes the fund a solid choice for investors seeking a higher level of income generation within their portfolio.

data.title: 4 Top Beaten-Down Tech Stocks
data.description:
Tech Stocks Down Amid Bubble Fears

Tech stocks have been hit hard due to AI bubble fears. Tech is down more than any other sector during the past 30 days as investors hope to regain some losses in a Santa rally in the final stretch of 2025.

S&P 500 Technology Sector (XLK) 1Y Price Performance

The S&P 500 and Nasdaq had reached record highs, led by technology stocks, before November’s selloff, as investors grew concerned over unprecedented AI spending, rate-cut uncertainty, sticky inflation, and weak employment data.

Fundstrat’s Tom Lee sees November’s selloff as a “healthy reset” and believes a dovish Fed could serve as a tailwind in pushing the S&P 500 as high as 7,300 by year-end. Market expectations for a December rate cut have jumped in recent weeks, with odds of a 25-bps cut at 89%. Lower interest rates tend to lift growth-oriented sectors like technology as investor appetite for risk increases and the value of projected cash flows rises.

While global AI-related investments are projected to exceed $4 trillion by 2030, an MIT study revealed AI initiatives in the first half of H225 have failed to produce measurable P&L returns for many companies.

Skepticism around AI, however, has overshadowed tech sector earnings performance and the upside of the technologies. Although trading well above historical averages, tech companies continue to post strong results. S&P 500 tech sector earnings grew 28% YoY in Q3 2025, with 93% of companies beating expectations, according to FactSet. And lost in the disappointment over near-term ROI are the rapid rates of AI adoption and early signs of higher labor productivity, which the Fed estimates has risen by roughly 1.3% since the introduction of ChatGPT.

The indiscriminate tech selloff has punished fundamentally sound companies, and SA Quant Ratings can help investors cut through the noise to pinpoint high-quality, undervalued stocks.

How to Select the Best Beaten-Down Tech Stocks

Seeking Alpha's Quant Ratings offer a data-driven evaluation of each stock by comparing more than 100 metrics to sector peers and assigning grades across five factors: Valuation, Growth, Profitability, Momentum, and EPS Revisions. Using the Seeking Alpha Technology Stocks Screener, I filtered for stocks with stable Strong Buy or Buy Quant Ratings that are down in the past 30 days.

1. Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL)

  • Market Capitalization: $91.11B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Technology Hardware, Storage, and Peripherals

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/3/2025): 15 out of 539

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/3/2025): 3 out of 28

Dell soared by 40% in the first 10 months of 2025 before falling 16% in the November selloff but recouped some losses after an AI-fueled earnings beat. Dell has been an unsung beneficiary of the AI boom—and comes without the bloated valuation - capitalizing on the surging demand for infrastructure solutions, which accounts for over half of revenue.

In addition to being the world’s third-largest PC vendor, Dell holds leading positions in the traditional server and storage markets, where growth is being driven by data center modernization, while its pipeline for AI-optimized servers continues to expand rapidly. Dell posted record third-quarter revenue of $27B, up 11% YoY, driven by robust demand for AI server shipments. Dell’s five-quarter pipeline continues to grow sequentially across Neoclouds, Sovereigns, and Enterprises and is several times larger than its $18.4B backlog. Dell expects full-year AI shipment revenue to grow by a staggering 150% to $25B and overall revenue to reach $111.7B (+17% YoY).

Strong return on capital and $7.1B in cash from operations underpin Dell’s ‘A’ Profitability Grade, while growth in earnings and operating cash flow in the past year drives a solid growth score. Dell is trading at a mere 13.71x earnings, a 42% discount to the sector median of 23.68x. Dell also showcases attractive valuation multiples across cash flow, operating income, and sales. A heavily weighted metric, PEG of 0.92, represents a 46% discount from the sector median.

Wall Street sell-side analysts have shown growing optimism over Dell’s financial forecasts, reflected in 18 upward earnings revisions in the past three months and one downward revision. Consensus estimates largely confirm Dell’s annual revenue guidance, while EPS is expected to grow by 21.85% this fiscal year and another 15% next year. Dell’s dividend yields 1.54%, just slightly above the sector median. A reasonable payout ratio of 22.10% supports a solid Dividend Safety Grade. Dell began paying a dividend in 2022 and has three consecutive years of payments. The lack of a long-term track record drags on its consistency grade. Dell’s dividend grew substantially over the past three years - at a CAGR of 26% vs. 6% for the sector - and is expected to continue growing at a solid rate in the next three. Dell is growing at a rapid rate with a leading position in the booming AI server market and offers investors a great potential buy-the-dip opportunity.

2. Hut 8 Corp. (HUT)

  • Market Capitalization: $4.07B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Application Software

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/3/2025): 12 out of 539

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/3/2025): 1 out of 179

No. 1 among quant-rated Application Software stocks and recently added to the PRO Quant Portfolio, Hut 8 is a vertically integrated Bitcoin miner that has diversified into AI/high-performance computing, power generation, and data center hosting, giving it additional income streams beyond crypto, as I pointed out recently in my Top Crypto Stocks article. HUT benefits from rising Bitcoin prices as well as increasing demand for AI infrastructure. Investors buying HUT must believe this AI transition will outweigh the risk, as its price has been highly tied to Bitcoin (BTC-USD). HUT has historically outperformed BTC in bull phases but has underperformed in downturns. HUT tumbled in the past month but has crushed the market, sector, and Bitcoin in the past year.

HUT vs. Bitcoin: 1Y Price Return

Hut 8 unexpectedly delivered a second straight quarter of profits in Q3, boosted by digital asset gains and robust compute revenue. HUT announced it is expanding energy capacity by 1,530 MW across four sites in the U.S. as part of a "power-first innovation-driven strategy," CEO Asher Genoot said in the earnings call:

"This initiative has the potential to more than double the scale of our platform, diversify our presence across strategic energy markets and position us to meet growing demand across energy-intensive applications. It underscores both the depth of our development pipeline and the scalability of our platform.”

HUT’s strong core profitability - including amazing EBITDA and cash flow margins - underscores its elasticity and ability to capitalize on the high-margin AI data center business. HUT also soundly outperforms in ROE and ROTC, while high capex/sales indicates the company is reinvesting at an impressive rate compared to the sector.

HUT’s robust core profitability is expected to continue with EBITDA FWD at +31%. Revenue was flat in the past year but grew by over 90% YoY in Q3 2025, driven by the expansion of Bitcoin mining. Revenue is projected to soar by 52% in FY25 and nearly 80% in FY26 to $446M. HUT has 10 upward earnings revisions and one downward revision. HUT’s valuation is strong relative to historical core profits, although future growth comes at a premium based on forward price-to-earnings and sales multiples. HUT’s sharp decline reflects a risk-off tone in the market - not the company’s fundamentals. HUT is well poised for a recovery as the price of Bitcoin stabilizes.

3. Viasat, Inc. (VSAT)

  • Market Capitalization: $4.54B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Communications Equipment

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/3/2025): 9 out of 539

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/3/2025): 3 out of 39

A global satellite network operator with mobility and government roots, Viasat serves military and commercial markets with high-speed satellite broadband services and secure networking systems. Viasat has 23 operational satellites in space, another eight under development, and recently launched the ViaSat-3 Flight 2, designed to deliver more bandwidth capacity than the entire existing fleet. Viasat’s networks have supported the surge in edge-based AI applications - from aviation and maritime autonomy to secure AI/ML workloads. VSAT has been on fire in the past year, with a scorching 1Y price return despite the recent dip, for an A+ Momentum Grade.

ViaSat’s Q2 of FY26 demonstrated continued strength in revenue across aviation, government satcom, IT security, and cyber defense markets. The company posted records in awards and backlog, which have reached $3.9B, while generating strong cash flow. ViaSat expects FY26 cash from operations to grow by double digits, while capex is projected to reach $1.2B, which includes $250M related to the completion of ViaSat-3. Once beyond these payments, ViaSat expects positive free cash flow for FY27. Sell-side analysts, meanwhile, project earnings to grow by 185% and revenue by 3% to $4.66B.

Viasat showcases solid EBITDA margins, while levered free cash flow surged in the past 12 months. The company’s top-notch Valuation Grade is driven by attractive EV/EBITDA and price/cash flow multiples, which are at 55% and 78% discounts to the sector. Viasat has a good chance to bounce back in light of exceptional earnings growth prospects, a record backlog, and a Strong Buy quant rating.

4. Xiaomi Corporation (OTCPK:XIACY)

  • Market Capitalization: $136.01B

  • Quant Rating: Buy

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Technology Hardware, Storage, and Peripherals

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/3/2025): 50 out of 539

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/3/2025): 7 out of 28

Another PQP stock, Xiaomi, ranked No. 3 among global smartphone shipments in Q3 2025 and saw robust growth in EV deliveries and IoT and lifestyle product sales. The Chinese-based electronics giant saw its stock slide in September when the company announced its new high-end smartphone chip would not be released annually, like Apple, but it bounced back last month on strong quarterly results and guidance.

Xiaomi's Q3 adjusted net profit rose 80.9% YoY and revenue by 22.3%, driven by a surge in EV deliveries. Smart EV, AI, and other initiative revenue climbed to record levels, up nearly 200% YoY. EV deliveries reached 108,796 vs. 39,790 in Q3 2024. Xiaomi expects to deliver more than 400K cars this year as the EV business becomes profitable at a faster pace than expected. According to consensus estimates, Xiaomi EPS is expected to grow by 62% in FY25 and revenue by 31% to $66.53B. The results support explosive performance in revenue, EBITDA, cash flow, and earnings in the past year, underpinning a solid Growth Grade, while robust operating and net income margins, along with strong return on equity and cash generation, support an A+ Profitability Grade.

At 21.7x earnings, the stock is trading at a slightly more attractive price than tech peers, but PEG TTM of 0.18 represents an 81% discount to the sector. Xiaomi was punished in a selloff and is now trading at an attractive price—a great candidate for wrapping up my list of top beaten-down tech stocks with strong underlying investment fundamentals.

Top Beaten-Down Tech Stocks Ready to Rally

Tech stocks have underperformed in the last month largely due to fears of an AI bubble, unprecedented capital spending, and concerns over no returns on investment. Lost amid the fear are massive earnings results posted by tech firms in Q3 2025, along with AI’s long-term upside as adoption and labor productivity rates rise. Moreover, the odds of a rate cut have risen dramatically, which bodes well for tech, while some analysts see the November selloff as a healthy correction. SA Quant identified four tech stocks beaten down in the last 30 days that possess strong underlying fundamentals, spanning the application software, hardware, and communications equipment industries.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top "strong buy" Quant stocks. Seeking Alpha’s quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

If you’re looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. It’s a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps. Happy investing!
