data.title: Nutrien: At A Pivotal Moment I Plan On Capitalizing On
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I’ve always thought the fertilizer industry was fascinated because it could move so much season to season, quarter to quarter. One of the companies that has always been on my watchlist is Nutrien Ltd (NTR) , the topic of today's article. I have been invested for about 2 years now but I think the company despite its impressive YTD performance can provide further upside. The tailwinds include increased US demand in nitrogen fertilizers and potash demand from both China and India. Depleting inventory levels further support elevated commodity prices. As I initiate coverage I’m glad I'm doing so with a Strong Buy.

Stock Chart 5-Year Performance

Over the past 5 years the performance of Nutrien has been mixed. It rallied during 2022 when the war in Ukraine broke out and global agricultural producers quickly bought up fertilizers to help ensure global demand could be met. This was because Ukraine is one of the largest grain producers in the world, among other agricultural items as well.

Since the merger in 2018 between PotAsh Corp and Agrium which created Nutrien, it also created what is now the world's largest potash producer and agricultural retail seller. As for nitrogen, it's the third largest in the world. When we look at the price over the past 5 years NTR has underperformed compared to peers like The Mosaic Company (MOS) or CF Industries Holdings (CF) significantly. These companies aren’t with the exact same products as NTR, but they do overlap in certain areas and are all operating within the larger fertilizer industry.

Since the merger the share price has risen by 7.9% as of the time of writing this. One share would have provided $15.43 in dividend payouts since then, bringing the total return for an investor buying January 8th 2018 a combined return of about 35.5%. You can see how that is well below what would be considered a modest return.

What newer investors perhaps miss is that NTR and its peers are highly cyclical companies where you need to consider where in the cycle we are and invest accordingly. In 2018 the market was in decent shape but the pandemic and the war meant global supply distribution. Much of this was quelled in the years after as commodity prices also fell, but we are now beginning to see a similar setup as in 2018 I think. NTR noted tight inventory levels in several key markets in the last earnings call and with how the sweeping tariff policy from the US has reshaped global trade, another similar price rush as in 2022 might come sooner rather than later.

Vertically Integrated Production Means Margins

One of the biggest advantages of NTR is the vertically integrated setup it has. NTR owns the upstream (mines, plants), the midstream (distribution network) and the downstream (retail sales). Despite being one of the most dominant fertilizer companies in the world it hasn’t necessarily equaled a strong stock price development, as I discussed earlier.

It operates in four distinct segments with a majority of the assets being in Canada, which is also where its headquartered.

Potash

The first segment is potash. It’s along with the next 2 here a part of the upstream and midstream operations of NTR. The company owns six mines in the Saskatchewan region in Canada, which also happens to be the world's largest region for soft-rock potash mining and production.

We maintained our 2025 global potash shipment forecast of 73 to 75 million tonnes, reflecting the upward revision made last quarter. We expect continued global potash demand growth and forecast global potash shipments between 74 and 77 million tonnes in 2026 supported by favorable affordability and low projected channel inventories in major markets. We anticipate limited new global capacity additions in 2026 with announced project delays.

The company maintained that it sees a continued uptrends for export levels for its potash production in the last report, building upon the raised guidance issued in Q2. It comes at a time when the company is shifting away from North American sales and going more international. During the first 9 months of 2025 the offshore sales rose from $1.316 billion to $1.75 billion this year. North American sales however remained flat at $1.449 billion, showing a slight decline from $1.479 billion last year. This means that offshore sales are now a majority of the potash total sales. This is the headlines story for sure, but what’s not mentioned is just how quickly NTR was able to reroute its distribution lines and supply global demand instead of North American demand.

In a report from the USDA it stated the country had about 220 million of metric tonnes in reserves but only produced about 480,000 tonnes per year, the bulk of that being in New Mexico. Now these might sound like big figures, but NTR exports that amount in a few years, which is why the US is also only accounting for 1% of the global production. Accelerating the production would only deplete the reserves faster and make it more reliant on foreign countries instead, which goes against the current administration strategy I think.

Revenues here are derived by mining potassium chloride which then gets processed into the granular fertilizer also called potash. During the last quarter average net selling prices rose to $277 per tonne, up from $213 last year and above the combined first 9 months this year of $250. This implies that prices are still rising, supported by both tight global inventory levels but also a renewed demand from China. This explains why NTR recently announced its goal of building a new potash terminal in the US at Longview in Washington. It would be valued at $1 billion and provide additional coverage and market access to the Asia-Pacific, specifically China, Japan and India.

As the table above shows, higher net selling prices combined with higher volumes has had a considerable impact on margins and EBITDA during the last quarter and YTD. It’s positive to see the COGS rising at a far lower rate as well, which helps amplify the margin expansion so far.

Nitrogen Sales Strong on the Back of High Usage in the US

In 2022, the top five fertilizers in the United States based on shares of the total metric tons of nutrient produced were ammonia (37.8 percent), phosphoric acid (16.3 percent), phosphate rock (15.4 percent), urea (13.3 percent), and ammonium nitrate (7.9 percent).

In the USDA report I cited earlier it also included figures on the current US fertilizer usage where ammonia topped the board at 37.8%.This also happens to be one of the strongest performing segments for the company right now, included in the nitrogen segment.

Despite some issues relating to the company's Trinidad and Tobago operations, which involves unreliable gas supplies leading to production shutdown. management did mention how strong US operations will be offsetting these otherwise lost volume outputs.

Going over the product line for the nitrogen segment it shows exactly what I’ve been talking about earlier, a large shift in fertilizer usage in the US. Farmers are pivoting towards ammonia and urea rather than potash instead, which is why this segment has outperformed NTRs potash segment so much.

Phosphate - Potential Segment Sale in 2026

I won’t go too deep into the third segment which is the phosphate segment. In the last report the company announced plans that it might be selling off the segment next year after a full review is completed. The argument for the potential sale is to simplify operations and create more long-term value for shareholders.

The segment has a combined asset value of $2.535 billion and generated $122 million in EBITDA last quarter and $275 million YTD. Estimating it will bring in about $400 million for the full year the EBITDA/Asset return is 15.77%. This only seems good when measured against the EBITDA because the depreciation is so high, $212 million YTD. Gross margins are far slimmer, sitting at 7.2% YTD and 5% for operating margins. If over 70% of the EBITDA is from adding back depreciation, well then it underscores the issues with profitability in the segment quite well.

Eliminating this segment would mean lower EBITDA for the company, the quality of that EBITDA is perhaps not what you’d want to have, if I can put it that way. I’d much rather EBITDA be made up of far less depreciation added back and instead higher operating income.

The comparison shows a clear underperformance in the phosphate segment. I think we can expect a value of at least $2.5 billion in case of a potential sale. The company already has a very stable balance sheet where total assets are nearly 2x the value of the total liabilities. Most of the asset value is in property and goodwill. Cash is $624 million so the proceeds from the sale could help fund the new potash terminal for example which is expected to have a $1 billion price tag as mentioned earlier.

Retail

Despite falling in sales YTD compared to 2024 I think that the retail segment showed a lot of strength during the last quarter, especially seeing as the adjusted EBITDA rose 5% instead. During Q3 however results across the board was positive and the Adj EBITDA margin rose by 210 bps to 6.7%.

Looking at the product mix we can see that the cause for the falling revenues seems tied to lower crop protection products and seeds products too. Interestingly, During Q3 the latter has increased by 18.1% YoY, going against the YTD trend. Management announced in the last earnings call additional products here so perhaps we can see this trend continuing into 2026.

We're going to introduce seven new nutritional products and several seed treatment products.

If trade talks between the US and China improve I think that the sales will grow rapidly in this segment as NTR won’t have to pivot to international markets which often have lower average selling prices, as opposed to a premium market like the US. President Trump did exempt earlier tariffs on fertilizer products like certain nitrogen and phosphate products in November. Tariffs on ammonia remain uncertain still however.

Forecasting Prices and Determining Fair Value

NTR does not explicitly state the sales of products by country. But I can make some fairly accurate assumptions. Offshore potash sales have continued to rise during the year. Crop nutrients, which to a large degree is dominated by nitrogen products, fell in terms of international sales. Because of this I think NTR will continue to realize higher selling prices as its more premium market (the US) becomes a larger portion of its core segment.

My theory is that the US will continue to pivot towards nitrogen based fertilizers like ammonia and urea whilst other larger economies like China and India will go towards potash instead. It’s quite reasonable because of the production mix in these countries. In the US you produce a lot of corn, this is a very nitrogen hungry crop, often needing about 150lb/acre and needs to be reapplied each year. Wheat and soybeans are also large agricultural items, both also very nitrogen hungry. With trade relations being slightly better between the US and China, American soybean production might be in better shape than initially anticipated, which supports rising nitrogen demand here.

On the contrary, China and India are far bigger producers of rice, vegetables and fruits which use potash instead of nitrogen fertilizers. The new terminal for the potash segments makes even more sense when considering this.

All three segments have shown considerable momentum in prices over the past 12 months as global trade has been disrupted and inventory levels are depleting fast. What we need to consider as well is the increase in gas prices since last year, rising from $2.35/MMbtu to $3.93/MMbtu. A rule of thumb is that a $1 change in gas prices either increase or decrease the input cost for nitrogen production by $30 - 35 per tonne as it uses on average 33 MMbtu per tonne of ammonia produced.

Same for sulfuric acid as well since that is an input cost for the phosphate production as well. Tracking the increase of this against the average selling price of phosphate explains why the segment continues to struggle, rising faster than sales prices.

The volume forecasts are the midpoint of the guidance which the management has laid, updated and reaffirmed as of the last quarter.

I’ve excluded the retail segment since that has remained more or less the same at $13 - 14 billion, and I think a 5 - 10% increase over the next 12 months is reasonable. The segments that are more volatile are the other 3. I’ve laid out 4 different scenarios which produce a revenue range of $7.568 - $11.296 billion with a midpoint of $9.432 billion which is close to where I think we’ll land next year.

The YTD EBITDA margin for all segments excluding retail was 48.6%. If prices continue to rise as well and the production too, in order to reach that midpoint range then EBITDA margin will likely expand as well. I’’ll be even and estimate 50%, giving me a combined EBITDA of $4.716 billion next year. Adding back the retail segment which has a EBITDA margin of 9.8% YTD. At 7% YoY revenue growth rate the 10% EBITDA margin retail would add $3.073 billion in EBITDA, or $7.789 billion. I think this is a reasonable assumption given the amount of volatility in the commodity prices. The estimate suggests prices stay above current levels for next year which I think is reasonable given the macro climate I’ve talked about. If input costs from natural gas and sulfuric acid rise I expect the EBITDA impact to be in the range of $250 - $350 million, in other terms, not catastrophic.

EV/EBITDA Valuation - Suggests Considerable Upside if NTR can Execute on Demand

The capital structure of NTR is largely propped up by debt which has increased over the past few years as the company invests in new production and assets. With the amount of EBITDA it generates the debt size is easily serviceable. The current EV is $42.61 billion. Based on my earlier projection the 2026 EV/EBITDA multiple would be 5.47. Considering NTRs 5-year average is 6.75 and the sector median is 8.9 I think we as investors are getting a great entry price here still, despite the 34% shares having risen YTD.

My fair value multiple is between the 5-year average and sector median, i.e., 7.85. The implied upside is around 30 - 35%, making shares a strong buy right now.

Risk

Commodity prices and shifting demand patterns remain the biggest risks for the company at this time. Further escalations or reinstatement of tariffs against fertilizers would undermine my bullish thesis here. With more free trade NTR could access premium markets like the US far easier rather than having to shift nitrogen sales elsewhere.

For potash demand it seems to remain stable from both India and China, but any shift here would again undermine the thesis I have made. What could trigger a shift is other regions or countries increasing production. It’s not impossible, Canada produced 32.4% of all potash in 2023, Russia 18.6% and Belarus 14.9% and China 9.3%.

Potash reserves are by far the largest in Canada, but remains substantial in the previously mentioned producers as well.

Production levels for Belarus are conflicting it seems, but what isn’t undisputed is the large reserves the country has of potash. My point here being that it could be a long-term threat against Canadian potash production with current production levels leaving it with nearly 200 years of reserves. If investments poured in it could triple this and be at the same or above Canada. Both China and India need to secure potash to help ensure they have reliable crop yields, this scenario isn’t entirely farfetched I think. It would likely bring down the potash prices as well. It should be said that this is not a near term risk but rather a long-term risk instead.

The Full Picture

I couldn’t make a short when covering such a comprehensive company like Nutrien. If it sells its phosphate business I think it would come back to investors as a special dividend perhaps or be fully utilized to fund the new production sites and assets management plans on buying. No matter which, the prices remain attractive as the market continues to show a lot of strength. My theory about increased nitrogen sales in the US might lead to higher average sales prices over the next 12 months if tariffs aren’t re-implemanted and US-China talks are positive. Again, no matter NTR trades at a considerable discount price, a price I plan on capitalizing on by increasing my initial stake in the company. Rating NTR shares a Strong Buy.

data.title: Planet Labs Prepares For Q3'26 Earnings Amid Strategic Quantum Systems Collaboration
data.description:
Thesis

Planet Labs (PL) earnings are just around the corner and set to come in sometime on the 10th of December. The stock has actually pulled back quite a bit since mid-October, as have many others in the same industry. Investors now are hoping the company can pick up a bit of sales momentum going into 3Q26. One of the ways we see surveillance companies do this is via a wide product base. Now, Planet Labs already holds quite a complete satellite lineup, with different satellites targeted at different surveillance needs. So, the best way to expand their customer base is via partnerships. And that's exactly what we're seeing with the latest Quantum Systems partnership. They're a European-based drone company, and I see their newly combined surveillance product helping boost sales in Europe, as I'll explain. Going into earnings, I see more upside ahead for Planet Labs stock, despite the October pullback.

What's Wall Street expecting

3Q26 earnings for Planet Labs are set to come in sometime next week, around the 10th of December. In terms of expectations, most analysts expect Planet Labs to again report a small quarterly loss when it releases earnings next week. The consensus calls for an EPS loss of just -$0.04, slightly deeper than the -$0.02 recorded a year earlier. Revenue, on the other hand, is projected to remain pretty solid, as you would expect, with a consensus of $72.02 million, which would reflect a pretty healthy 17.6% year-over-year growth. As a quick recap, management said they expect this quarter's revenue to land somewhere between $71 million and $74 million and forecast the full FY26 revenue to be in the range of $281 million to $289 million. In my opinion, Planet Labs seems to be broadly on track to meet that full-year revenue target. However, it still needs solid performance over the next two quarters to get there. Quarterly revenue is now in the low-$70 million range and growing around 17% to 20% year over year, by my observations. So the company is pacing in line with guidance. Most importantly, however, it can't afford a slowdown if it wants to finish within the guided range.

What also doesn't allow room for any slowdown is the stock momentum. Shares have seen very good momentum over the past year on the back of defence- and AI-related tailwinds. The stock is up about 204% over the past 6 months, and despite Liberation Day, the stock is still up 189% year to date. Now, the way I see Planet Labs hitting that FY26 guided figure is by a bigger product base. As you know, Planet Labs currently has a very high-tech satellite constellation. Among the industry, their resolution numbers for their imagery are right at the forefront of the competition. So to expand that customer base, they widened the type of satellite available to customers. You can check out my previous coverage for an overview of their satellite lineup. Now, what's quite common for players in this industry is when they need to expand their product appeal, we often see them partner with other companies. And that's exactly what Planet Labs has started to do. A big driver of sales will come from the European defence market, and a good way to boost sales on that front is to do something different in terms of surveillance.

A Partnership With Quantum Systems

As you've probably heard, Planet Labs and Quantum Systems have formed a partnership. It's aimed at exploring/developing integrated "Tip & Cue" intelligence solutions for their European defence customers. It's set to blend wide-area satellite monitoring with some pretty rapid, high-precision drone reconnaissance. Now, Quantum Systems is a European developer of AI-powered unmanned aerial systems. They specialise in autonomous, military-grade drones, and their tech covers a wide array of integrated mission software.

The goal here is to take Planet's broad-area change-detection capabilities and their very-high-resolution satellite and combine it with Quantum Systems' MOSAIC UXS software and advanced multi-sensor unmanned aerial systems. The two companies have some kind of a workflow outcome in mind in which AI automatically flags significant environmental/operational changes from orbit and then cues targeted drone missions for close-range confirmation and analysis of what's really going on down on the ground. This is extremely high-tech and actually takes a lot of coordination. The whole concept is designed to conserve scarce drone assets, so it only sends out drone teams when there is something to actually check out. The workflow should also streamline intelligence cycles and reduce any cognitive load on analysts since AI will be flagging the activity. Once a drone team is dispatched, it should, in theory, deliver more detailed, actionable insights more quickly to operators in the field. One of the biggest overall goals of surveillance companies is to capture quality data; the better the quality, the easier it is for a decision maker to take action.

That's what customers want and why high-resolution satellites have been in such demand this year. Now, Planet Labs is taking it a step further and saying, if you don't have the resources to investigate all the anomalies on the ground that we flag for you, there is an automated drone option to deliver another load of secondary high-quality data. It truly is astonishing the workflow they aim to create, and it's something I feel could be in high demand across Europe, an area Planet Labs has worked hard to boost sales in. Now, both companies frame the partnership as an extension of their products for European security and Ukraine's defence. They mentioned that having a tighter integration between space-based and on-the-ground aerial intelligence will be far safer, more efficient, and more precise for decision-making for their military customers.

As a quick example, imagine Planet Labs satellites see some newly cleared ground, unexpected vehicle tracks, or even structures appearing overnight. This will create an automated "Tip." The alert then flows directly into Quantum Systems' MOSAIC UXS mission software, which has the job of identifying the nearest available drone to the activity areas and then preparing a precise mission plan to investigate the location. A Quantum Systems drone would then launch and fly autonomously to the coordinates. The drones use multi-sensor payloads to capture more high-resolution visual, thermal, or infrared imagery. They are essentially analysing the scene in real time to confirm whether the anomaly is an actual security concern. Everything then gets transmitted back to analysts and commanders within minutes.

Valuation

Planet Labs is currently trading at quite a rich valuation compared to the sector. It's no secret the market is willing to pay a bit of a premium for the stock, given just how advanced their surveillance tech is among peers and tailwinds for AI in defence. On a trailing twelve-month basis, the stock holds a price-to-sales ratio of 13.54x, far above the sector median of 1.76x. And an EV to sales of 12.99x compared with a sector median of 2.21x. The company also holds a market cap of $3.66 billion. In terms of cash, their position is pretty strong at about $271.54 million and a low total debt of $17.54 million.

Looking Ahead

Going into earnings, investors should keep an eye out for that year-over-year growth figure and should see it remain above 17% for the company to be on target for that FY26 guidance. I'm also expecting management to give us an early update on the demand in Europe and explain more about the potential from the Quantum Systems partnership. The stock has been on a slow decline leading into these earnings, but I think we should see things turn around going into 2026. All in all, I remain buy-rated on Planet Labs.

Crypto investors felt like they were tossed through a washing machine in November, as relentless selling pressure shook coins loose from weaker hands. The result was that Bitcoin (BTC-USD) reached the lowest 30-day Relative Strength Index (RSI) readings (~32) since the de-peg of Lido ETH in June 2022. RSI measures the speed and magnitude of recent price changes to identify overbought or oversold conditions. BTC, ETH, and SOL (SOL-USD) each fell (-23%), (-27%), and (-31%). Crypto selloffs were heavily concentrated during US trading sessions, which contributed roughly 85% of November BTC losses.

Though the Coinbase Premium Index, which tracks Coinbase’s BTC price premium to non-US exchanges, briefly turned positive over Thanksgiving weekend, the index was negative for the vast majority of November. The “flush” in November 2025 somewhat mirrors the one crypto experienced after Trump’s tariffs in April 2025, when BTC fell from $109K to $76K. The result of recent price action is that (~55%) of Bitcoin’s supply is in profit, which marks the lowest reading since September 2023. In the previous bear market, Bitcoin supply in profit reached a low of (~31%) in November 2022 after the collapse of FTX.

MarketVector Smart Contract Leaders Index (MVSCLE) Fell by -25% in November

Open interest in crypto futures continued to decline from October, reaching the lowest levels, $29B, since the chaos of the tariff market reaction in April 2025. BTC ETP outflows in BTC terms were (-2.5%), while ETH ETP redemptions corresponded to (-8%) of AUM measured in ETH. The extreme levels of uncertainty drove 30-day trailing volatility to the mid-40s, approaching the lower levels seen in April 2025, when volatility averaged above 50. Besides broader concerns about AI spending and Federal Reserve policy, crypto traders worried about emerging narratives around quantum computing affecting Bitcoin encryption, Digital Asset Treasury (DAT) weakness, and selling by ancient whales.

Cumulative BTC Returns by Session 10/24 - 11/24

This dismal backdrop translated into weak onchain fundamentals, with blockchain revenues down (-37%) m/m to reach ~$200M in November. DEX volumes across all chains were down (-26%) m/m and (-35%) y/y. Meanwhile, Hyperliquid gained more market share of blockchain earnings, reaching (40%) of the market to gross $80M on the month. After the conclusion of the perpetual future DEX mania of October, BNB’s revenues have fallen (-76%) m/m, dropping it to fifth place in blockchain revenues. Stablecoin (SBC-USD) transfer volumes were down (-19%) m/m across all chains, but still showed y/y growth of (+54%). In that category, Ethereum (ETH-USD) continues to dominate, posting roughly the same volumes as the next four competitors combined.

Hyperliquid Revenue Gains as BNB Fades

Stablecoins on blockchain also pulled back after reaching an all-time high of 308B in October, declining to 304B at the end of November. Though USDC lost (-2%) or 1.2B of its market capitalization, Ethena’s USDE lost (-30%) m/m or nearly (-50%) off its mid-October high (14B-$7B). The decline in overall stablecoins partly relates to the compressing rates environment for crypto. The best gauge of blockchain yields, the trailing 30-day perp funding rates, fell as low as (3.8%) after averaging (7-8%) over the summer. These yields reflect crypto traders’ willingness to take directional risk, and current levels are the lowest since October 2023. With respect to Ethena, the substantial decline is most likely attributable to the peg break it experienced due to market chaos on October 10, when some exchanges priced USDE as low as $0.65 rather than $1.00. Another bit of bad news for stablecoins came at the end of the month when S&P downgraded Tether’s rating for peg stability from 4 (constrained) to 5 (weak), which is S&P’s lowest rating.

As an ending note, we would like to remember one of the best F1 drivers of all time, Ayrton Senna. Ayrton relished rainy race days because wet conditions amplified his bold, precise skills, allowing him to overcome substantial deficits to win races. When other drivers dialed back their risk tolerances, Ayrton increased his and benefited enormously. Chaotic times offer immense opportunities for the disciplined investor. While we do not know when this downpour will end, we are carefully positioning our portfolios for the sunny times to come.

30-Day Correlation BTC/Nasdaq Broke 1-Year Highs

Top Crypto Questions from Clients This Month

1 - Are bitcoin whales selling?

Bitcoin Spent Volume from 5+ Year Holders (30-Day Moving Average)

We have seen a modest uptick in selling of older cohorts of BTC holdings. As we can see in the chart above, this selling by older cohorts is far below earlier peaks. The largest reductions in positions have occurred in the 2-5-year holding band. This cohort has dropped in total BTC holdings by (-31%) since November 2023. These holders appear to be positioning based on their interpretation of Bitcoin’s place in the 4-year cycle. Zooming out, while the percentage of supply >5 years old has remained consistent at (30.5%), we have seen a massive drop in % the supply >2 years old category, moving from (57%) in January 2024 to (48%) today. All the while, short-term active supply, <6M Old, has surged from (22%) in June 2025 to (31%) today, which is the highest level since July 2021.

2 - Is it too late to buy bitcoin?

In our opinion, it is difficult to call any level ‘too late’ for BTC, and these levels may reflect short-term panic more than a long-term view of fundamental potential. BTC recently experienced a greater than (-30%) drawdown, which mirrors past pullbacks during bull markets in 2017 and 2021.

3 - Is crypto in a bubble?

Some crypto tokens certainly need to be repriced, including those associated with ghost blockchains and low-utility applications. However, we believe there are still worthwhile investment opportunities for those with a very high risk tolerance.

4 - Have we passed the price peak in Bitcoin's 4-year cycle?

Many 4-year cycle forecasters divine that we have already reached the price apex of this cycle. However, lower volatility and the explosion in real-money involvement suggest a smaller drawdown than previous cycles. Thus, VanEck bought the dip in model portfolios at ~$80K on Friday, November 21st, seeing an attractive risk/reward, with technical indicators signaling a near-term bottom. However, we remain flexible and not dogmatic about the cycle with a healthy respect for the 4-year patterns.

The underlying dynamics of the 4-year cycle may have changed as Bitcoin miners diversified their income streams and institutional players viewed BTC as a unique macro asset. That noted, many in the crypto community, including large whales, believe that the Bitcoin 4-year cycle is infallible. If these parties act, they will likely weigh on prices.

Index Definitions

S&P 500 Index: is widely regarded as the best single gauge of large-cap U.S. equities. The index includes 500 leading companies and covers approximately 80% of available market capitalization.

Nasdaq 100 Index: is comprised of 100 of the largest and most innovative non-financial companies listed on the Nasdaq Stock Market based on market capitalization.

MarketVector Centralized Exchanges Index: designed to track the performance of assets classified as 'Centralized Exchanges.'

MarketVector Decentralized Finance Leaders Index: designed to track the performance of the largest and most liquid decentralized financial assets, and is an investable subset of MarketVector Decentralized Finance Index.

MarketVector Media & Entertainment Leaders Index: designed to track the performance of the largest and most liquid media & entertainment assets, and is an investable subset of MarketVector Media & Entertainment Index.

MarketVector Smart Contract Leaders Index: designed to track the performance of the largest and most liquid smart contract assets, and is an investable subset of MarketVector Smart Contract Index.

MarketVector Infrastructure Application Leaders Index: designed to track the performance of the largest and most liquid infrastructure application assets, and is an investable subset of MarketVector Infrastructure Application Index.

MarketVector Digital Assets 100 Large-Cap Index: market cap-weighted index which tracks the performance of the 20 largest digital assets in The MarketVector Digital Assets 100 Index.

MarketVector Digital Assets 100 Small-Cap Index: market cap-weighted index which tracks the performance of the 50 smallest digital assets in The MarketVector Digital Assets 100 Index.

MarketVector Meme Coin Index: modified market cap-weighted index which tracks the performance of the 6 largest meme coins. Meme coin refers to crypto assets often named after characters, individuals, animals, artworks, or other memetic elements. Initially supported by enthusiastic online traders and communities, these coins are intended for entertainment purposes.

Coin Definitions

Bitcoin (BTC): A decentralized digital currency enabling peer-to-peer transactions without intermediaries or a central authority.

Ethereum (ETH): A decentralized smart-contract platform used to build and run applications and Layer-2 networks.

Solana (SOL): A high-throughput Layer-1 blockchain; SOL is used for fees and staking to secure the network.

BNB (BNB): The native asset of BNB Chain used for transaction fees, staking, and ecosystem utilities.

USD Coin (USDC): A fiat-backed U.S.-dollar stablecoin issued by regulated partners, designed to maintain a 1:1 USD peg.

Tether (USDT): A widely used fiat-backed U.S.-dollar stablecoin intended to hold a 1:1 USD peg.

Ethena USDe (USDE): A synthetic U.S.-dollar stablecoin from Ethena that targets a ~$1 value via a delta-neutral backing model; market price can trade above/below the peg during stress.

Lido Staked Ether (stETH): A liquid staking token representing staked ETH on Lido that accrues staking rewards and is redeemable for ETH over time.

Hyperliquid (HYPE): The native token of the Hyperliquid L1 + perps DEX, used for governance, staking/fee-sharing, and protocol incentives.

data.title: 2026: A Rare Alignment Of Policy, Liquidity, And AI
data.description:
Thesis Summary

We're less than 30 days away from 2026, and we are entering the year with a macro setup unlike anything we've seen in years.

President Trump has confirmed he will announce his new Federal Reserve Chair "early next year."

Treasury Secretary Bessent has stated that "substantial tax refunds" are coming in Q1 2026.

Together with rate cuts, other forms of monetary and fiscal stimulus, and the acceleration in the AI spending cycle, you have a recipe for a good year in stocks.

The message for investors has been clear since the day Trump got elected. Investors need to own assets.

2026 is shaping up to be the year policy, liquidity, and sentiment finally align.

A New Fed Chair and a New Regime

Trump's comment that his new Fed Chair will be announced "early next year" is one of the most important macro signals of the year.

Odds are strongly in favour of Kevin Hassett.

But what matters is that a Trump-appointed chair is overwhelmingly likely to prioritise:

  • Lower rates

  • A more dovish stance on inflation

  • Full employment and growth over restrictive policy

  • Coordination with fiscal authorities rather than conflict

Even before a new chair takes over, markets will begin pricing an easier Fed. That alone could be enough to shift behaviour across credit, equities, crypto, and risk assets.

Not Just Rate Cuts

Finally, the Supplementary Leverage Ratio (SLR) has been modified. Behold, the eSLR.

Regulators have signed off on a long-sought easing of capital rules for the largest U.S. banks, cutting the extra leverage cushion tied to Treasury holdings in a move meant to free up balance sheets and encourage more government bond buying.

The change centers on the enhanced supplementary leverage ratio or eSLR and reflects a Trump administration push to dial back post-crisis constraints and draw more bank demand toward longer-term Treasurys such as the 10-year note.

Source: Globest.com

While this has largely gone unnoticed, it's a policy that I flagged months ago and something that should go a long way to ease capital and help lower yields.

Q1 2026: Treasury-Driven Liquidity Wave

Treasury Secretary Bessent's comment that "substantial tax refunds" are coming in Q1 should be well-received by the market

Refund season is already one of the largest natural liquidity injections of the year. In 2026, that injection is expected to be earlier and larger

Refunds hitting household accounts in Q1 function exactly like stimulus.

They increase spending, raise consumption, and improve the earnings backdrop for corporates.

They also drive immediate flows into markets.

Q1 often sets the tone for the entire year, and a liquidity-positive Q1 historically leads to double-digit annual equity performance.

Stimulus Checks Are Not Off the Table

While nothing formal has been announced, the political incentive is powerful.

Combine high interest costs, a high cost of living, and an administration that seeks to deliver visible wins early, and the probability of targeted stimulus payments rises significantly.

Even modest stimulus checks can meaningfully influence:

  • Consumer confidence

  • Retail activity

  • Corporate revenue numbers

  • Market sentiment

  • Fund flows

A similar, though perhaps smaller, dynamic in 2026 could supercharge risk assets at a time when AI, semiconductors, and infrastructure spending are already in full expansion mode.

The AI Supercycle Adds Fuel

The global AI build-out is continuing at historic speed.

Hyperscalers are committing tens of billions per quarter in capex, and the industry is entering the inference and commercial deployment phase.

And again, the Trump admin has a hand in here, something I talked about in my last piece on the AI bubble.

The Genesis Mission will set the government up to be a big player in AI, providing not just a backstop, but also fueling investment and animal spirits.

In a year when policy is easing and fiscal flows are rising, AI acts as the structural tailwind that supports higher multiples.

2026 will be the first full year when both AI and liquidity move in the same direction.

How Should You Invest?

So, which are the sectors best positioned to benefit from the 2026 macro regime:

1. Mega-Cap Tech & AI Platforms

Of course, mega-caps are still poised to outperform. Not just because of their exposure to AI, but also, importantly, because they make a substantial amount of revenue from abroad, which also makes them a great dollar hedge.

2. AI Infrastructure & Data Centers

Though people talk of a bubble, I think we are early in the cycle, with credit easing and the government stepping in to further help these companies.

3. Bitcoin & Crypto

Historically, Bitcoin follows liquidity with near-perfect correlation. Rate cuts and refunds alone are bullish. Add the potential for stimulus, and it becomes rocket fuel.

True, this doesn't play out with the four-year cycle, but this hasn't played out the way it was supposed to.

Risks

No macro environment is without risk.

Unexpected inflation re-acceleration would be a big problem for the rate cut narrative.

There's also a risk of global shocks (energy, geopolitics, or supply chain disruptions)

And though most believe the Fed and Trump will now align, there's room for some more drama in this narrative, which could shake markets

Final Thoughts

The coming year presents an alignment that investors rarely get:

  • A new Fed chair

  • Rate cuts likely

  • A massive Q1 liquidity injection from tax refunds

  • Potential stimulus on the horizon

  • Ongoing fiscal expansion

  • An AI capital spending supercycle

  • Improving global liquidity conditions

For long-term investors, the message is simple. Keep adding, and make sure you own assets. As always, the way I see it, the bigger risk here is staying in cash.

data.title: RingCentral: The Operating Leverage Effect Still At Work
data.description:
Since our last review of RingCentral's (RNG) earnings report, the company's shares have fallen by 4% as the S&P 500 has risen by 7%. To be honest, we have not found any clear prerequisites for such a dynamic of quotations relative to the market. However, we believe that all of our key arguments remain valid. The company continues to ramp up its profitability, which could lead to a revaluation of its stock in the future.

3Q earnings

- Revenue climbed by 5% y/y to $638.7 mln (~$3 mln above consensus);

- EBITDA jumped by 50.7% y/y to $87.2 mln. We calculate EBITDA as operating income + D&A;

- Subscription revenue went up by 6% y/y to $616 mln

- ARR totaled $2,630 mln (+6% y/y);

- The number of AI Receptionist users rocketed by 85% q/q to 5,800+ clients;

- RingCX (an AI-powered contact center solution) has been in place for as long as a year, with the number of customers rising by 150% to more than 1,350 over the time;

- The AI Conversation Expert (the new name for RingSense) expanded by 250% y/y to 4300+ customers;

- Customer retention holds at about 99%. We estimate that ARR retention reached 99.3%, an increase from 98.7% in 2Q.

Management’s guidance

The guidance for revenue growth in 2025 has been tweaked to the downside, or from 4%-6% to 4.5%-5%. But the FCF forecast has been raised again, from $515-$520 mln to $525-$530 mln, which at the moment translates into a FWD FCF Yield of ~19.5%. GAAP operating margin is expected to reach 4.8%-5% in 2025.

Interpreting the earnings report

The report slightly beat expectations, but the shares fell at the time of its publication, probably due to a general market correction, along with the downward revision of the 2025 revenue range in the guidance. However, the shares subsequently retraced some of the losses. It should be noted that subscription revenue is growing at a normal rate of 6% per year. The slowdown is in “other income,” which is not segmented affects about 3% of total revenue.

On the plus side, following a consistent slowdown in the growth of subscription revenue (which spanned three straight quarters), we are finally seeing an acceleration, albeit a slight one (from 5.6% y/y in 2Q to 5.7% y/yin 3Q). The company has probably reached a plateau, and we believe the slowdown will halt in the future, with the pace of growth stabilizing.

The company introduced a new product, AVA (AI Virtual Assistant). It is an agent that helps optimize and automate tasks during customer interactions. RingSense was also renamed AI Conversation Expert (ACE). Management emphasized that these products are already beginning to make a significant contribution. The target for AI products remains the same: $100 mln in ARR by the end of 2025. According to the earnings call transcript, CEO Vladimir Shmunis announced the company's transition to RingCentral 3.0, or a new era of agentic voice AI. The company is evolving from a UCaaS provider to a multi-platform communications provider, where AI agents are integrated at all stages of customer interaction.

RingCentral is strengthening its position in healthcare, finance, retail, and professional services. Voice traffic on the platform is growing at double-digit rates, with users making tens of millions of calls per day (~100 mln minutes), reaffirming the stability of the core voice business. Joint sales with GSP partners (operators), primarily AT&T, are picking up speed: the GSP channel already makes up more than 10% of total revenue and is expanding at a double-digit pace, on track to achieve operating profitability in less than 18 months.

Meanwhile, the company's profitability is improving at a rapid clip. As a reminder, our main pitch for RNG was not about astronomical revenue growth but operating leverage. We are currently seeing this in the form of a 1.5x y/y increase in EBITDA, as well as a second straight quarter of positive GAAP net income. RingCentral also reported positive earnings for the first nine months of 2025 ($20.4 mln).

Optimization of expenses as a percentage of revenue, as well as working capital management (primarily reducing accounts receivable and managing prepaid/deferred expenses for sales agent commissions), has had a positive impact on cash flows: The company has raised its FCF forecast in each quarter of the current financial year.

That means the company continues to ramp up profitability as its revenue growth stabilizes. The subscription segment is expected to deliver a growth of 5.5%-6%. We believe that the business is developing in a positive direction overall.

Additionally, as part of our model:

- We see a positive trend in ARR retention (we define this indicator as annualized revenue for the current quarter divided by ARR for the same quarter of the previous year). We have explained our methodology in a previous article. So, ARR retention in the previous quarter was 99.3% compared with 98.7% in 2Q. This was the first increase after three straight quarters of declines. We believe that new AI products will prompt a recovery in retention to above 100% in 2026 (it remains to be seen whether this will fully materialize).

- Adjustments have been made to the target multiple calculation, as more than half of 2025 is now behind us. The calculation now uses expected EBITDA growth in 2026/2025 versus 2025/2024 previously. Growth will be slower in 2026 due to a high base. In calculating the target multiple, we use the base EV/EBITDA multiple multiplied by the EBITDA growth rate for the following year. We calculate the base EV/EBITDA as the average EV/EBITDA of a handful of companies in the industry divided by their EBITDA growth rate by year.

Valuation

We are raising the EBITDA forecast from $315.7 mln (+41% y/y) to $327.4 mln (+45% y/y) for 2025 due to the higher-than-expected actual level in 3Q 2025 and to the recovery of ARR retention. Our forecast for 2026 is $401 mln (+22.5% y/y).

We are lowering the target price from $72 to $67 on account of the changes in the calculation of the target multiple, which went down from 21х to 18.3х). We are maintaining the rating at BUY.

As a reminder, RingCentral's valuation is subject to increased volatility, as the company is in the middle of a transition period and has yet to achieve stable profitability.

Risks for the investment case remain the same as in the initial coverage article:

  • Strong competition. RingCentral operates in a fairly fragmented market, holding a market share of less than 5%. The company's key competitors are Zoom, Microsoft, and Cisco. These are major players with significant resources to increase their own share of the UCaaS market.
  • Volatile financial performance. RingCentral has just come near the operating break-even point. The investment case is built on the assumption that management will continue to optimize operating expenses while ramping up revenue. A reversal of the trend (for example, OPEX starting to rise again as a % of revenue) would result in a significant downward revaluation of the stock.

Bottomline

RingCentral’s stock has lagged the market despite solid operational progress, but the core thesis remains intact: profitability is accelerating, cash flow is strengthening, and ARR retention is stabilizing. AI-driven products are gaining real traction, subscription growth has likely bottomed, and operating leverage is now clearly visible. While near-term revenue guidance softened and valuation remains volatile, rising EBITDA expectations support continued upside potential.
